case_description: [1] In the cross-border region between North Rhine-Westphalia in Germany and Wallonia in Belgium, [2] a growing number of small businesses such as bakeries, craft workshops, and repair services face closure due to the lack of suitable successors. [3] The regional governments of North Rhine-Westphalia and Wallonia, concerned about the potential long-term deterioration of local economic activity, employment, and service provision, [4] decided to take coordinated action. [5] Given the shared language and integrated regional economy, [6] the two governments opted to jointly implement a public measure to support the transfer of existing businesses to new owners. [7] To this end, they created the Euregion Succession Fund, [8] a jointly administered body tasked with implementing the measure and managing the associated resources. [9] The fund operates under arrangements established by both governments and receives its financing from their respective budgets. [10] EUR 125 million was allocated from North Rhine-Westphalia and EUR 75 million from Wallonia. [11] These amounts were paid into a central fund account overseen by a joint committee. [12] The committee is composed of senior officials from both regions and takes all grant decisions under jointly agreed procedures. [13] The fund provides non-repayable grants covering up to 15 percent of eligible business transfer costs for undertakings operating in the defined cross-border region. [14] To qualify, applicants must meet certain criteria relating to residence, business continuity, and local employment retention. [15] GutesBrot GmbH, a German bakery chain, applied for a grant to take over several bakeries in Eupen. [16] After confirming compliance with the eligibility criteria, the committee approved a grant of EUR 300,000. [17] The amount was disbursed from the fund’s account to cover legal fees, inventory purchases, and refurbishment costs.
